turns.content: My hollandaise broke again. What’s the actual science behind emulsions and why do they fail? I’m tired of vague “don’t overheat it” advice.

Hollandaise is a warm, acidified oil-in-water emulsion stabilized mainly by egg yolk components. The “science” version is: you’re trying to suspend a large volume of liquid fat as tiny droplets in a continuous water phase, and you need (1) enough emulsifiers to coat those droplets, (2) enough shear to create them, (3) the right viscosity/temperature so droplets don’t collide and merge, and (4) a protein network that doesn’t over-coagulate or under-structure. When it “breaks,” one of a few failure modes has happened: 1) Coalescence: oil droplets merge into bigger droplets until you see oily separation. This happens if droplets aren’t coated well (not enough emulsifier, too much oil added too fast, not enough shear) or if the continuous phase gets too thin/hot so droplets collide and merge more easily. 2) Protein coagulation/curdling: egg proteins denature and clump. That disrupts the emulsion because you lose a uniform stabilizing matrix and water phase. You’ll see graininess or scrambled-egg bits, often accompanied by separation. 3) Phase inversion: you accidentally push it toward water-in-oil (rare in hollandaise because water content is relatively low but can happen if the water phase is extremely low and you add oil aggressively or reduce too far). The texture changes suddenly. 4) “Starvation” of water phase: if you evaporate too much water (over a hot bain-marie, or starting with too little water), the continuous phase becomes too concentrated/viscous in a way that seems thick at first, but it can’t accommodate the oil droplets properly; then it can’t buffer temperature well either. The stabilizers in yolk: lecithin (a phospholipid) plus proteins. Lecithin is amphiphilic: one end likes water, one end likes fat. It adsorbs to the oil-water interface and lowers interfacial tension, making it easier to create and keep small droplets. Proteins also adsorb and form a film; with gentle heat they denature partially and increase viscosity, which helps keep droplets apart. Too much heat and they coagulate into curds. So “don’t overheat it” is shorthand for “don’t force yolk proteins into irreversible aggregates and don’t thin the water phase so much that droplet collisions and coalescence outpace stabilization.” If you tell me exactly how you made it—bowl type, heat source, whisk method, how fast you added butter, clarified or whole butter, starting liquid (water/lemon/vinegar reduction)—I can map your break to a specific failure mode.

Fine. I did the classic: bowl over a saucepan with simmering water. Two yolks, a splash of lemon, whisked until foamy, then I started adding warm clarified butter. It thickened, then suddenly went thin and greasy. No curds, just oily. I wasn’t blasting heat. I was whisking like crazy. Why would it go from thick to broken so fast?

That “thick then suddenly thin/greasy with no curds” is textbook coalescence collapse—droplet growth crosses a threshold where viscosity drops and free oil appears. It can happen abruptly because emulsion stability isn’t linear: once droplet size distribution shifts upward, collisions accelerate and you get a cascade. Likely causes in your exact setup: 1) Butter added too fast relative to emulsifier availability and shear Even if you whisk “like crazy,” there’s a limit to how fast you can create new interface area. Each new droplet needs surface coverage by lecithin/protein. If you dump in butter at a rate that creates more new interface than emulsifiers can cover, droplets partially coalesce. It can look fine briefly because viscosity is rising as droplet volume fraction increases; then as larger droplets form, the emulsion loses that “mayonnaise-like” structure and thins. 2) Temperature drift upward mid-process A bain-marie can be deceptively hot because steam and bowl conduction can spike the yolk layer temperature. As the sauce thickens, it insulates itself; the bottom layer can get hotter than you think. Higher temperature reduces continuous-phase viscosity and increases droplet mobility → more collisions → more coalescence. Also, higher temperature can weaken the interfacial protein film (proteins can unfold but not yet gel), making it less elastic. 3) Water phase too low Two yolks + “a splash” of lemon may be low in water. Hollandaise needs enough aqueous phase to be continuous and to dissolve/keep proteins mobile. Classic technique often starts with a reduction (vinegar/water) or at least 1–2 tsp water per yolk. If your water phase is marginal, the emulsion can be fragile: it thickens quickly (high dispersed-phase fraction) then breaks once you exceed what that water phase can “hold.” 4) Butter temperature too high If your clarified butter is very hot, it warms and thins the sauce locally, creating transient low-viscosity zones where droplets merge. Also, very hot fat can denature proteins at the interface in a way that reduces their ability to stabilize (not necessarily curdling, more like weakening the film). Why it’s sudden: emulsions can be metastable. You’re balancing creation of small droplets vs. their tendency to merge. If the rate of coalescence overtakes the rate of droplet breakup and stabilization, the system flips. A quick diagnostic question: when it broke, was the bowl still on heat, and was the butter stream steady (like a thin ribbon) or more like intermittent pours?
Bowl was still on the heat. Butter was a thin stream at first, then I got impatient and poured more. But I thought once it’s thick it can handle more butter, not less.

Thickness is misleading because it comes from having lots of droplets packed together, not from “strength.” A thick emulsion can be closer to the edge. As you add butter, you increase the dispersed-phase volume fraction (oil droplets). Viscosity rises because droplets hinder flow—until you exceed the capacity of the emulsifier/water phase to keep them separated. Think of it like inflating balloons in a box: at first everything gets tighter (thicker), but at some point balloons pop and merge into big blobs (break). Two key limits: 1) Emulsifier coverage limit Total interfacial area scales with droplet size. If you keep droplets small, you create a massive interface area that must be coated. Yolk emulsifiers are finite. If you add fat too fast, droplets form and collide before being fully coated. Once partially coalesced, they become larger droplets with less total surface area—but the damage is done because the structure that provided thickness is gone and free oil can appear. 2) Continuous-phase adequacy limit Even with enough emulsifier, you need enough water phase to be continuous and viscous enough. As oil fraction rises, the water phase becomes a thin “film” between droplets. If it’s too thin, droplets touch and coalesce. Adding more butter pushes you toward that point. So the “impatient pour” while still on heat is a perfect storm: higher temperature lowers viscosity and speeds collisions, and faster addition outruns emulsifier adsorption. If you want a robust process: pull the bowl off heat once the yolks have thickened (or keep the bain-marie barely warm), add butter slowly until the emulsion forms, then you can slightly increase pace—but not by much—then adjust with water/lemon to keep the continuous phase healthy.

You keep saying “enough water.” But classic recipes don’t add water beyond lemon juice. Is water actually part of the structure? And how does acid factor in? I’m not asking for recipe tips; I want the mechanism.

Water is not optional in an oil-in-water emulsion; it’s the continuous phase. In hollandaise, the continuous phase is an aqueous solution containing dissolved salts, acids, and dispersed/solubilized yolk proteins and phospholipids. Lemon juice contributes water, but the amount may be small and variable. Mechanistically, water affects: 1) Continuity of the phase For oil-in-water, water must be the continuous medium. If the aqueous fraction is too low relative to oil, you approach close packing of droplets and risk inversion or coalescence because droplets are forced into contact. 2) Viscosity and collision frequency Higher continuous-phase viscosity slows droplet movement and reduces collision/coalescence. Egg proteins (partially denatured) increase viscosity, but they need water to hydrate and form a network. If there’s insufficient water, proteins can’t create a smooth viscous matrix; you get a thick paste that can’t “cushion” droplets. 3) Heat buffering Water has high heat capacity. A more substantial aqueous phase resists temperature spikes. With very low water, the sauce temperature can jump quickly with small heat input, destabilizing the emulsion. Acid’s roles (lemon juice/vinegar): 1) Protein charge and solubility Proteins have charged groups; pH changes their net charge. Near their isoelectric point (where net charge ~0), proteins are less soluble and more prone to aggregation. Egg yolk proteins have complex behavior, but generally mild acidification increases positive charge on many proteins, increasing electrostatic repulsion between protein-coated droplets and improving stability—up to a point. Too close to certain pH ranges or too high ionic strength can reduce solubility. 2) Denaturation temperature shift Acid can lower the temperature at which some proteins denature/coagulate. That sounds bad, but in hollandaise you want partial denaturation for thickening, not full coagulation. Acid can make that thickening happen at slightly lower temperatures, giving a wider perceived “working range” if you’re careful. But it can also make overheating more punishing. 3) Flavor aside, it can affect interfacial film properties Proteins at the interface form a viscoelastic layer; pH influences how they unfold and cross-link. A more elastic film resists droplet coalescence. So: water provides the medium; acid tunes protein behavior and droplet interactions. When recipes omit “water,” they’re often relying on (a) water naturally in yolks, (b) water in lemon juice, and (c) water that remains if you use whole butter (which includes water). Using clarified butter removes butter’s water, making your “water budget” tighter—so you may need to add a bit of water explicitly to keep the same stability.
That’s interesting: clarified butter removes water. So is clarified butter actually worse for hollandaise stability? Everyone acts like it’s the gold standard.

Clarified butter is “cleaner” in flavor and removes milk solids that can scorch, but from a purely emulsion-stability standpoint it can be less forgiving because it reduces the aqueous fraction you’d otherwise get from whole butter (which is typically ~15–18% water) and removes some surface-active milk components. Whole butter contains: - Fat (continuous when melted) - Water droplets (dispersed in the melted fat) - Milk solids (proteins, lactose) When you add melted whole butter into yolks, you’re effectively adding both oil and a bit of water. That extra water can help maintain an oil-in-water emulsion because it increases the continuous-phase volume once it migrates into the aqueous phase. It also moderates how quickly the sauce thickens (lower oil fraction per unit butter added), giving you more room. Clarified butter is nearly pure fat, so each tablespoon pushes oil fraction up faster. That makes addition rate and initial water content more critical. Also, milk proteins can be weak emulsifiers. They’re not the main stabilizers in hollandaise, but they can contribute marginally. Removing them can make the system more dependent on yolk emulsifiers. So “gold standard” is mostly about predictability and avoiding scorched solids, not necessarily maximum stability. Many professionals still prefer clarified because they manage water/temperature well. For a home cook, whole butter can be more forgiving—unless the extra water later causes thinning if you add too much liquid overall. It’s a balance.

Okay, but why does it sometimes break even when I add butter slowly? I’ve had batches where I was patient and it still separated near the end. Is there a point where the yolk just can’t hold more fat no matter what?

Yes. There’s a capacity limit, and it depends on droplet size distribution, emulsifier concentration, and continuous-phase volume/viscosity. Two relevant “ceilings”: 1) Maximum dispersed-phase volume fraction (packing limit) Even perfectly stabilized droplets can only occupy so much volume before they’re forced into contact. For monodisperse hard spheres, random close packing is ~64%. Emulsions can exceed that because droplets deform, but as you approach high oil fraction, the continuous phase becomes thin films between droplets. Any disturbance, heat, or insufficient viscosity can cause film rupture and coalescence. Hollandaise is often in the range where oil fraction is high enough that it’s inherently near this limit, especially if water is low and butter is clarified. 2) Emulsifier-to-interface ratio Total interfacial area A scales roughly as: A ~ (oil volume) / (droplet radius) Smaller droplets → bigger A → more emulsifier needed. If your whisking creates very small droplets early, you might “spend” emulsifier capacity coating lots of interface. Later, when you add more oil, there may not be enough emulsifier left to cover new interface. Then droplets coalesce, which reduces A, but you can get macroscopic separation during the transition. This is why sometimes a sauce looks beautifully thick (small droplets, high A) but is actually fragile if emulsifier is near saturation. The “near the end” break can also be temperature-related: as you keep the bowl over heat longer, water evaporates slowly, concentrating the sauce. It thickens, you think it’s stable, but the water phase is shrinking and viscosity behavior changes. Then one more addition of hot fat pushes it over the edge. If you want the mechanism in one sentence: stability requires the rate of droplet stabilization (adsorption + film formation) to exceed the rate of droplet collision/coalescence; near the end, higher oil fraction and sometimes lower water content increase collision/coalescence probability and reduce the margin.
You mentioned droplet size. I always thought smaller droplets are better. Are you saying too small can be bad?

Smaller droplets are generally better for kinetic stability (they cream less, they feel smoother), but they demand more emulsifier because they create more interfacial area. So “smaller is better” only holds if you have enough emulsifier to fully coat and stabilize that interface. In hollandaise, emulsifier supply is limited (two yolks only contain so much lecithin/protein). If you create extremely fine droplets early, you maximize interface area and can approach emulsifier saturation sooner. Later additions of oil then produce insufficiently coated droplets that coalesce. There’s also a practical point: whisking intensity and viscosity interact. Early on, the mixture is thin, so whisking can generate high shear and create small droplets. As it thickens, your whisking may become less effective at breaking droplets, so new oil added later may form larger droplets that are less uniformly stabilized, increasing polydispersity and destabilization pathways. So it’s not that small droplets are intrinsically bad; it’s that there’s a tradeoff between droplet size and emulsifier capacity. The best emulsion is one where droplet size is small enough for texture but not so small that you run out of emulsifier for the intended oil load.

That makes sense. Another thing: sometimes I get this weird texture where it’s thick but kind of spongy or clumpy, not smooth. It’s not scrambled eggs, but it’s not glossy either. What failure mode is that?

That sounds like partial protein aggregation without full curdling—think of it as micro-gelling or localized overcooking. Mechanistically: - Egg yolk proteins begin to denature with heat. Denaturation exposes hydrophobic regions, and proteins can aggregate via hydrophobic interactions and sometimes disulfide bonds (more relevant in whites, but yolk has some). - In a properly controlled hollandaise, you want partial denaturation to increase viscosity and help create a stable continuous phase. But if parts of the sauce exceed the target temperature (hot spots at the bowl bottom, or hot butter hitting a small area), you can get localized aggregation. - Those aggregates can form tiny gel particles. They’re too small to look like “scrambled eggs” but large enough to scatter light and disrupt the glossy, continuous appearance. They can also trap droplets unevenly, creating a clumpy/spongy mouthfeel. This can coexist with an emulsion that hasn’t fully broken. In fact, it can be a precursor to breaking because the smooth interfacial stabilizing environment becomes heterogeneous. Common triggers: - Bowl too close to boiling water (steam heat can be intense). - Not lifting the bowl on/off heat frequently. - Butter too hot, added in a way that creates thermal spikes. - Thin sauce early on: hot spots affect a larger fraction quickly.
So the bowl-over-simmering-water setup is inherently risky because of hot spots? I thought that was the gentle method.

It’s gentle in average temperature but can be uneven in instantaneous, local temperature—especially with thin mixtures. A bain-marie provides indirect heat, but: - Steam is ~100°C at atmospheric pressure. The bowl surface can approach that, especially if it sits close and the water is actively simmering. - The sauce layer in contact with the bowl is thin and can heat quickly. - If you whisk continuously, you average temperatures, but any lapse or thicker sauce can let the boundary layer overheat. So it’s “gentler than direct flame,” but not automatically “safe.” The gentleness comes from controlling the water temp (barely steaming, not simmering hard), keeping the bowl a bit above the water, and modulating contact time. Some cooks prefer direct low heat with constant whisking because it’s more responsive; others prefer bain-marie because it caps the maximum temperature somewhat. Either can work; the key is avoiding localized overheating and evaporation.
I want to go deeper on “emulsifier adsorption.” Is it instantaneous? When I add butter, does lecithin immediately coat the new droplets, or is there a time lag where they’re vulnerable?

There’s a time component. Adsorption isn’t instantaneous; it’s fast, but not infinite-speed, and it competes with droplet collision. What happens when you introduce oil into an aqueous phase with emulsifiers: 1) Droplet formation by shear Your whisk creates disruptive forces that tear oil into droplets. Immediately after formation, the droplet surface is “bare” oil-water interface with high interfacial tension. 2) Emulsifier diffusion/transport to interface Emulsifiers (lecithin molecules, proteins) must reach the interface. Small molecules like lecithin diffuse relatively quickly; proteins move slower and may need to unfold to anchor. In a viscous medium, diffusion is slower. 3) Adsorption and rearrangement Once at the interface, emulsifiers adsorb and often rearrange to pack efficiently. Proteins may partially unfold to maximize contact. This takes time. During the interval between droplet creation and full coverage, droplets are vulnerable to: - Coalescence upon collision (bare interfaces merge easily) - Partial coalescence (especially with fats that can crystallize; less relevant at hollandaise temps, but if butter is not fully melted or sauce cools, it can matter) So yes, if you add oil faster than emulsifiers can cover new interface, you create a population of under-protected droplets that coalesce. That time lag is one reason “slow drizzle” matters, especially early when emulsifier concentration relative to new interface is tight and the system is thin (droplets move and collide more readily).
You said “proteins unfold.” Isn’t that denaturation? I thought denaturation is bad.

Denaturation is a spectrum, not a binary “good/bad.” In emulsions, partial denaturation of proteins can be beneficial. - Native (folded) proteins may be less surface-active because hydrophobic residues are buried inside. - Partial unfolding exposes hydrophobic regions that can anchor at the oil-water interface, making proteins better emulsifiers. - Some unfolding in the continuous phase also increases viscosity, which stabilizes the emulsion by slowing droplet movement. The “bad” zone is extensive denaturation leading to aggregation and gelation into curds. That’s when proteins bind to each other more than they bind to the interface or the aqueous phase, forming clumps and expelling water/fat. So hollandaise is essentially exploiting controlled denaturation: enough to thicken and stabilize, not so much that it curdles.
If that’s true, then why do some recipes tell you to whisk yolks off heat with lemon until thick, then add butter without heat at all? Wouldn’t you need heat to denature proteins properly?

You can get a stable hollandaise-like emulsion with minimal additional heat because: 1) Mechanical shear can help proteins adsorb and unfold at interfaces even without much bulk heating. The interface itself is an environment where proteins can unfold due to surface forces. 2) The yolks may already be warm from initial thickening (or from warm butter), and you may not need more heat once the emulsion is established. 3) Lecithin (phospholipids) doesn’t require heat to function; it’s an effective emulsifier at room temperature. Heat is a tool to: - Increase viscosity via protein denaturation - Help incorporate butter smoothly by keeping fat liquid - Increase rate of emulsifier adsorption/unfolding But once you’ve achieved a stable droplet network and sufficient viscosity, additional heat can become more risk than benefit. That’s why many techniques shift from heating to maintaining warmth.
Let’s talk about “breaking” versus “splitting.” Are those different scientifically or just different words?

Mostly different words used interchangeably, but you can map them to different dominant failure modes: - “Split” often implies visible separation into oil and watery phase: classic coalescence and phase separation. - “Broken” can mean split, or it can mean curdled/overcooked with graininess. Scientifically, you’d classify by: 1) Coalescence-driven phase separation (oil droplets merge, oil layer forms) 2) Protein aggregation/gelation (grainy curds, sometimes with separated liquid) 3) Inversion (rare here; the continuous phase flips) In kitchen language, people say “broken” for all of the above, which is why advice gets vague.
I’ve heard that adding a teaspoon of cold water can “fix” a breaking hollandaise. How can water fix an emulsion that’s already failing? That sounds like superstition.

It’s not superstition; it targets the continuous-phase problem. If your hollandaise is starting to separate due to too-high oil fraction or too-low water/viscosity, adding a small amount of water can: 1) Increase continuous-phase volume This increases the thickness of the aqueous films between droplets, reducing direct droplet contact and slowing coalescence. 2) Lower viscosity in a controlled way that improves shear effectiveness Counterintuitive: if the sauce is extremely thick (high droplet packing), whisking may not generate enough effective shear to break up droplets or redistribute emulsifier. A tiny bit of water can make it more workable so whisking can re-disperse oil. 3) Reduce temperature slightly If it’s on the edge due to heat, a bit of cooler water pulls it back. But it won’t fix everything. If the yolk proteins have curdled significantly, water won’t re-solubilize them. If there’s already a large pool of separated oil, water alone may not re-emulsify unless you also provide shear and an emulsifier-rich phase to capture that oil. Mechanistically it’s like “rebuilding the continuous phase” so existing emulsifiers can do their job again.
What about fixing with another yolk? People say start a new yolk and whisk the broken sauce into it. Why does that work better than water?

Because it adds emulsifier capacity. A broken hollandaise often fails because: - There isn’t enough emulsifier to stabilize the amount of oil present (or the emulsifier is compromised). - Or the droplet size distribution has shifted toward large droplets and free oil. Adding a new yolk provides fresh lecithin and proteins, increasing the available emulsifier-to-interface ratio. When you whisk the broken sauce (oil + some water) into the new yolk, you’re essentially re-emulsifying the oil into a new, emulsifier-rich aqueous phase. Water helps if the main issue is continuous-phase shortage; a yolk helps if the main issue is emulsifier shortage. Often both are contributing, which is why the “new yolk” fix is more reliable.
Can we quantify any of this? Like, is there a rough ratio of butter to yolk that’s stable? Or is it all dependent on droplet size and technique?

There are rough practical ranges, but the true limit depends on droplet size, water content, and how much of the yolk’s emulsifiers are available (which varies with yolk size and handling). Conceptually, the maximum oil you can emulsify scales with: - Amount of emulsifier (lecithin + proteins) - Desired droplet size (smaller droplets require more emulsifier) - Presence of other emulsifiers (milk solids) and stabilizers (viscosity from proteins) In practice, classic hollandaise ratios often land around: - 1 yolk per ~50–75 g butter (sometimes more) But you’ll see recipes pushing higher butter per yolk if they include a water/vinegar reduction and manage technique tightly. If you want a “physics” lens: you’re trying to keep oil fraction below the point where the aqueous films become too thin and to keep total interfacial area within emulsifier coverage. Without lab measurements, you can’t compute it precisely, but you can infer when you’re approaching the limit: sauce becomes very thick, then starts to look slightly greasy at the edges, or it loses gloss—those are signs the interface is stressed.
You mentioned interfacial tension earlier. Does whisking actually lower it, or is that only emulsifiers? What’s whisking doing besides mixing?

Whisking provides mechanical energy to create new interface by breaking oil into droplets. It doesn’t lower interfacial tension by itself (interfacial tension is a material property of the oil-water interface), but it helps emulsifiers do their job by: 1) Creating droplets (increasing total interface area) 2) Distributing emulsifiers quickly throughout the system so they can reach new interfaces 3) Preventing localized overheating by moving fluid 4) Preventing droplet flocculation/coalescence by keeping droplets separated Lowering interfacial tension is primarily the emulsifier’s role. However, vigorous shear can transiently deform droplets and promote emulsifier adsorption by constantly renewing interface and preventing droplets from sitting and merging. So whisking is “creating the problem” (more interface) and “enabling the solution” (emulsifier access and distribution).
Another angle: why does hollandaise feel thicker than mayonnaise sometimes, even though mayo can take way more oil? Is the structure different?

The structure and rheology differ because of temperature, fat state, and the role of proteins. Mayonnaise: - Usually a cold oil-in-water emulsion - Stabilized strongly by lecithin and proteins in yolk, plus often mustard and sometimes other stabilizers - Oil is liquid, droplets are stable; viscosity comes from high oil fraction and droplet interactions Hollandaise: - Warm emulsion - Fat may be fully liquid, but temperature is near where butterfat fractions behave differently; still, mostly liquid at serving temp - Viscosity comes from oil droplets plus a partially denatured protein network in the aqueous phase (a “custard-like” thickening component) - Continuous phase can be more viscous due to protein denaturation, giving a different mouthfeel—more “saucy” and elastic, less “cold spread” So hollandaise can feel thick at a lower oil fraction because the continuous phase is thickened by heat-modified proteins. Mayo relies more purely on droplet packing and sometimes added stabilizers. Also, mayo often uses neutral oils and can be built to extremely high oil fraction with very small droplets if emulsifier is sufficient and technique is right. Hollandaise is constrained by warmth and by butter’s composition and temperature sensitivity.
If mayo can handle more oil, why can’t hollandaise just be made like mayo—no heat, just yolk + lemon + butter blended?

You can, and people do (immersion blender hollandaise). But classic hollandaise aims for specific properties: - Warm serving temperature - Flavor/texture from gently cooked yolk (slight thickening, reduced “raw yolk” notes) - Stability at warm temp for some period If you make a cold emulsion of yolk + lemon + melted butter, you still face: - Butter must be liquid to incorporate; if it cools, fat crystallization can destabilize or make it grainy. - Warmth reduces viscosity and can destabilize the emulsion compared to cold mayo. - Without controlled heating, you may not get the same protein-thickened continuous phase, so it may be thinner at the same oil fraction. So “like mayo” works as an emulsion method, but the final sauce’s stability and texture depend on managing temperature and protein state. The blender method often compensates with very fine droplet size and rapid emulsification, which can be stable enough if served quickly.
You keep mentioning fat crystallization. In hollandaise it’s warm, so why would crystallization matter at all?

It matters mainly during transitions—if any component is cool enough, or if the sauce sits and cools. Butterfat is a mixture of triglycerides with a range of melting points. Even around “warm” temperatures, some fractions can begin to crystallize if the sauce drops toward the low 30s °C range (depending on composition). If crystals form: - They can protrude through droplet interfaces, promoting partial coalescence (crystals act like bridges between droplets). - They can create a grainy texture (“buttery” granules). - They change droplet mechanics: partially crystalline droplets are less deformable and can destabilize differently. Classic hollandaise, kept properly warm, minimizes this. But if your kitchen is cool, your bowl is cold, or you pause and the sauce cools, crystallization can contribute to breakage or graininess.
Let’s say my sauce broke into an oil slick plus some watery yolk base. If I whisk hard enough, shouldn’t it just re-emulsify? Why does it sometimes refuse?

Because re-emulsification requires both sufficient emulsifier and sufficient shear under the right conditions. When it breaks into a slick: - A lot of oil has already coalesced into a continuous oil phase. - The remaining aqueous phase may be depleted of emulsifiers (many are stuck at interfaces of remaining droplets or denatured/aggregated). - The system may be too warm/thin, so droplets formed by whisking coalesce again immediately. - Or too thick/gelled in patches, preventing uniform droplet breakup. Whisking alone can re-emulsify if: - There’s enough active emulsifier remaining - The aqueous phase is adequate (water and viscosity) - Temperature is in a workable range - You add oil gradually (even if it’s “already there,” you can treat the re-emulsification as gradual by whisking in the broken sauce slowly into a small stable base) If it refuses, it usually means the emulsifier is insufficient or compromised. That’s why adding a fresh yolk (emulsifier) or a bit of water (continuous phase) changes the outcome.
I’m still annoyed because it feels like roulette. Can we map symptoms to causes more decisively? Like a decision tree.

Yes—symptom-based diagnostics: A) Looks grainy/curdled, like tiny scrambled bits; may also leak liquid Cause: protein coagulation from overheating or localized hot spots; possibly too little acid/water buffering. Fix path: cool slightly; strain if needed; rebuild by whisking into a fresh yolk + a little water (but curds won’t dissolve). B) Suddenly turns thin and shiny, with oil separating; no graininess Cause: coalescence from too-fast fat addition, insufficient emulsifier, too high temp (thin continuous phase), or too little water. Fix path: whisk in 1–2 tsp warm water; or start new yolk and slowly whisk broken sauce into it; reduce heat and slow addition. C) Was thick, then becomes greasy around edges; still mostly together Cause: approaching oil fraction limit / water shortage; early warning. Fix path: whisk in a teaspoon of water or lemon; reduce heat; slow butter addition. D) Sauce is thick but dull, slightly clumpy/spongy (not curds) Cause: partial protein aggregation/micro-gelling from borderline overheating or hot butter. Fix path: pull off heat; whisk vigorously; add a small amount of water to smooth; avoid hot spots. E) Sauce is runny from the start and never thickens Cause: insufficient emulsification (butter added too fast before base formed), yolks not warmed/structured, or too much liquid early. Fix path: start over or create a stable base with yolk + small water, then add fat slowly. If you tell me which of these matches your most common failure, we can narrow the root cause.

Most common is B: it thickens, then it suddenly goes thin and oily. So coalescence. But why would coalescence happen after it’s already emulsified? Shouldn’t the established droplet network protect it?

An established emulsion can still fail if conditions shift. During hollandaise making, conditions are constantly changing: - Oil fraction increases as you add butter. - Temperature can drift up. - Water can evaporate. - Emulsifier gets redistributed: some is locked at interfaces, leaving less in the continuous phase to stabilize new droplets. - Shear effectiveness changes as viscosity changes. So “already emulsified” just means you’ve built a stable region for a certain composition. As you add more oil, you’re moving along a stability landscape. You can cross a boundary where the emulsion becomes unstable. When you cross it, coalescence can accelerate quickly—hence sudden collapse. Also, droplet networks don’t “protect” against new oil additions unless those additions are incorporated as droplets and stabilized. If you add a lot of oil at once, you temporarily create large oil domains that must be broken up. If shear isn’t sufficient at that moment, those domains can merge with existing droplets and cause a chain reaction.
Is there a reason hollandaise recipes often say “add butter off the heat” but also “keep it warm”? That seems contradictory.

It’s two different phases of the process: - Building phase: You want control. Off heat reduces the risk of overheating and reduces evaporation. You can focus on emulsification—forming and stabilizing droplets. - Holding/serving phase: You want it warm enough to stay fluid and pleasant, and to keep butterfat fully melted. But “warm” is below the point where proteins coagulate further and below where viscosity drops too much. So: emulsify in a safer thermal environment, then maintain at a gentle warmth. The contradiction is only apparent if “keep warm” is interpreted as “keep heating.” It’s more like “don’t let it cool and set.”
You’re making it sound like hollandaise is a delicate colloidal system. Is there any hard science concept that captures “delicacy”?

Two concepts: kinetic stability and metastability. - Thermodynamically, an emulsion is usually unstable because the system lowers free energy by reducing interfacial area—i.e., by separating into oil and water. Emulsifiers lower interfacial tension and create barriers, but they don’t typically make the emulsion the true equilibrium state. - So emulsions are kinetically stable: they persist because there are energy barriers to droplet coalescence (steric barriers from emulsifier layers, electrostatic repulsion, increased viscosity). “Delicate” means those barriers are not huge. Small changes in temperature, composition, or shear can lower barriers or increase collision rates, tipping the balance. Hollandaise is particularly metastable because it’s warm (higher molecular mobility, lower viscosity), has high oil fraction, and its stabilizing proteins are near a denaturation threshold.
Does salt matter? Not for taste—does it change stability?

Salt changes ionic strength, which can affect protein behavior and electrostatic interactions: - Increased ionic strength can screen electrostatic repulsion between charged groups on proteins and between protein-coated droplets. Reduced repulsion can increase droplet flocculation and coalescence risk. - Salt can also affect protein solubility (“salting in” at low concentrations, “salting out” at higher). In culinary ranges, it can subtly change viscosity and protein interactions. In hollandaise, typical salt amounts are unlikely to be the primary stability driver, but dumping a lot early could marginally reduce stability by screening charges. Acid and temperature usually dominate.
What about mustard? People put mustard in “mock hollandaise” or béarnaise sometimes. Is mustard doing real emulsion work or is it just flavor?

Mustard can be a real emulsifier/stabilizer. It contains mucilage (polysaccharides) and some proteins that: - Increase viscosity of the continuous phase (thickening) - Provide additional surface-active components - Help stabilize droplets via steric effects In mayonnaise, mustard is a classic stabilizer. In hollandaise-family sauces, a small amount can increase robustness, though it also changes flavor profile and texture. Mechanistically it’s adding both emulsifier-like molecules and hydrocolloids that slow droplet movement and coalescence.
Let’s get concrete about temperature. People throw around numbers like 60°C, 65°C, etc. What’s actually happening across that range?

Roughly: - Below ~45–50°C: Butterfat is liquid, but yolk proteins are mostly native. Emulsification can occur via lecithin, but the sauce may be thinner and less “cooked” in texture. Stability can be okay if droplet size is fine and water phase is adequate. - ~55–65°C: Many yolk proteins begin to denature significantly. Viscosity increases; you get the classic nappé thickness. This can improve emulsion stability by thickening the continuous phase and strengthening interfacial films—up to a point. - Above ~65–70°C (ballpark): Risk of aggregation/curdling increases sharply, especially with localized hot spots. Once proteins aggregate into curds, texture becomes grainy and emulsion stability degrades. Exact numbers vary with pH (acid), salt, concentration, and heating rate. Gentle heating with acid can produce thickening at slightly lower temperatures but also can narrow the window before aggregation if you overshoot.
You’re cautious with numbers, which I respect. But if the window is that narrow, why do restaurants crank out hollandaise reliably? Are they just better at whisking?

They control variables systematically: - Consistent ratios (standardized butter/yolk/lemon and often a water/vinegar reduction) - Controlled heat sources (steam tables, induction, precise bain-marie setups) - Clarified butter at a known temperature - Often making a larger batch, which has more thermal inertia (less prone to spikes) - Using tools (blenders, thermometers, insulated containers) to reduce variability - Experience recognizing visual cues early Also, many restaurant hollandaise processes include a reduction (vinegar/water with aromatics) that increases the water phase and acidity in a controlled way, making the emulsion more forgiving than “two yolks + splash lemon + clarified butter.”
I didn’t do a reduction. I hate extra steps. But from a science view, a reduction is just controlled water and acid, right?

Exactly. It’s a way to: - Set a known amount of water in the system - Set a known acidity level - Concentrate flavors without relying on ambiguous “splash” - Provide dissolved solids that can slightly increase viscosity From a mechanism perspective, it’s pre-engineering the continuous phase so the emulsion has a stable foundation before you start adding oil.
I want to understand the “sudden collapse” more. Is there a mathematical model? Like a critical point?

In colloid science, you can describe stability with concepts like: - DLVO theory (electrostatic + van der Waals forces) for particle interactions—less directly applicable because protein/lecithin layers add steric effects and the droplets are deformable. - Critical volume fraction and percolation: as dispersed-phase fraction increases, droplets form a jammed network; viscosity rises dramatically. Small changes can cause large rheological shifts. - Coalescence kinetics: rate depends on collision frequency (function of droplet concentration, viscosity, temperature) and coalescence efficiency (probability a collision leads to merger, which depends on interfacial film strength and thickness of aqueous films). The “sudden” part is often due to nonlinear feedback: - As droplets coalesce, average droplet size increases. - Larger droplets rise/cream faster and collide differently. - The emulsion’s viscosity can drop (less droplet surface area interacting), increasing mobility and collisions. - That accelerates coalescence further. So you get a runaway process once a threshold is crossed—like an avalanche model more than a linear degradation.
If viscosity dropping accelerates coalescence, why does it drop when droplets get bigger? Intuitively bigger chunks should make it thicker.

In emulsions, viscosity isn’t just “chunks”; it’s about the number of droplets, their interactions, and surface area effects. - Many small droplets at high volume fraction create lots of interactions and a structured network; they resist flow because they’re numerous obstacles with lots of interface. - If droplets coalesce into fewer larger droplets, you reduce the number density and total interfacial area. The system can flow more easily, especially if the continuous phase is relatively thin. You can also create free oil that acts like lubrication. There are regimes where bigger droplets can increase apparent thickness (like a chunky sauce), but in a classic broken hollandaise, the hallmark is thinning because the fine droplet network collapses and you get separation.
So a stable hollandaise is basically a foam-like packing of tiny oil droplets in a thickened water phase?

That’s a good mental model. It’s like a concentrated emulsion with a structured continuous phase. The “thickness” comes from: - High dispersed-phase fraction (many oil droplets) - Droplet-droplet interactions (crowding/jamming) - Increased continuous-phase viscosity from partially denatured proteins And the gloss comes from uniform droplet size distribution and smooth continuous phase.
When people say “whisk until ribbons,” what are you actually looking for scientifically?

“Ribbon stage” in this context is a proxy for: - Partial protein denaturation and increased viscosity in the yolk-water-acid mixture - Incorporation of some air (foaminess), which also indicates viscosity increase and shear history A thicker base does two stabilizing things: 1) It slows droplet movement, reducing collisions. 2) It helps disperse incoming fat into smaller droplets because the base can “grab” and shear it more effectively. So “ribbons” is a qualitative indicator that the continuous phase is viscous enough to accept oil without immediate coalescence.
Air. Does air matter? I don’t want my hollandaise foamy.

Air isn’t necessary for stability, but it’s often introduced during whisking. Small amounts of entrained air can: - Increase apparent volume and lightness - Potentially destabilize if bubbles coalesce or if foam interferes with emulsion uniformity In practice, a bit of air early isn’t a big issue; the sauce usually de-foams somewhat as butter is added and as it sits warm. Excessive foaming can be a sign of aggressive whisking in a thin base; it’s not inherently bad, but it can make visual cues harder to read.
I’ve also had hollandaise that was too thick—almost like paste. Then when I tried to thin it with lemon, it got greasy. Why would thinning trigger greasiness?

Because adding acid/water changes both viscosity and interfacial conditions, and if you add it abruptly you can destabilize the droplet network. Possible mechanisms: 1) Local dilution and shear disruption If you add liquid in a small spot, you create a low-viscosity pocket where droplets can move and collide more easily, promoting local coalescence. If that local coalescence happens, it can seed broader separation. 2) pH shift affecting proteins A sudden pH change can alter protein charge and interfacial film properties. Usually mild acid helps, but abrupt changes can transiently weaken the network or alter solubility. 3) Overconcentration prior to thinning If it was paste-thick, you might have been beyond optimal oil fraction or had too little water. The emulsion may have been “barely holding.” Thinning reduces viscosity and can remove the jammed structure that was mechanically preventing separation, revealing that emulsifier coverage is insufficient. So thinning can be safe if done gradually with whisking, but a sudden addition can destabilize a borderline emulsion.
This is the first explanation that actually matches what I see. One more: sometimes I see little beads of butter forming on the surface while it’s still mostly stable. Is that coalescence starting?

Yes—those beads are often early-stage coalescence or “oiling off.” They indicate some droplets are merging and rising to the surface. It can be caused by: - Temperature too high (lower viscosity, weaker interfacial films) - Oil fraction too high relative to emulsifier/water - Insufficient shear at that stage (e.g., you stopped whisking while still on heat) - Butter added a bit too fast If caught early, it’s often recoverable by: - Removing from heat - Whisking to re-disperse - Adding a small amount of water to strengthen the continuous phase

You keep slipping into “how to fix.” I’m not mad, it’s just—I want to understand the core failure. Is coalescence always because of insufficient emulsifier, or can it happen even with enough emulsifier?

Coalescence can happen even with enough emulsifier if the protective barrier is compromised or collision rate is too high. Two levers: - Coalescence efficiency (probability a collision leads to merge) - Collision frequency (how often droplets collide) Even with adequate emulsifier, coalescence can occur if: - Temperature is high → lower continuous-phase viscosity → higher collision frequency - The interfacial film becomes less elastic or partially disrupted (e.g., proteins over-denature in a way that makes the film brittle) - Mechanical shock (sudden dumping of oil) creates large droplets/domains that are hard to stabilize - Aqueous films drain too quickly between droplets (thin continuous phase), allowing interfaces to touch and merge So insufficient emulsifier is common, but not the only cause. In hollandaise, though, emulsifier limitation often shows up because the system is concentrated and warm.
Are there any experiments I can do in my kitchen to isolate variables? Like prove to myself it’s water content or temperature?

Yes—controlled mini-batches: 1) Water content test Make two small batches with identical yolk and butter amounts. - Batch A: yolk + lemon only - Batch B: yolk + lemon + measured water (say 1–2 tsp per yolk) Keep butter addition rate and temp the same. Observe: stability margin, how fast it thickens, and whether it breaks near the end. 2) Butter temperature test Use clarified butter at two temperatures: “just melted warm” vs “very hot.” Keep everything else identical. Observe: tendency to sudden collapse and surface oil beads. 3) Heat exposure test Build the emulsion mostly off heat, then compare holding it over the bain-marie vs holding it in a warm spot. Observe: how quickly oiling-off occurs. 4) Addition-rate test Weigh butter and time your additions with a stopwatch. Observe: whether a faster rate correlates with breaking point. These aren’t lab-grade, but they make the causal links visible.
Suppose I want to model hollandaise as a system: components, phases, interfaces. Can you describe it like a materials scientist would?

Sure. System: a concentrated, warm oil-in-water emulsion with protein/phospholipid emulsifiers and a partially gelled/viscous aqueous continuous phase. Phases: - Dispersed phase: butterfat droplets (liquid triglyceride mixture) - Continuous phase: aqueous solution (water from yolk + lemon/vinegar + any added water; dissolved salts/acids; dispersed yolk proteins and phospholipids) - Minor phase: possible air bubbles (if whisked), and possibly tiny solid fat crystals if temperature drops Interfaces: - Oil-water interfaces coated with a mixed layer of phospholipids (lecithin) and adsorbed proteins. The layer is viscoelastic and provides steric and sometimes electrostatic stabilization. Stabilization mechanisms: - Reduced interfacial tension and interfacial film formation (emulsifiers) - Increased continuous-phase viscosity (protein denaturation) - Electrostatic repulsion modulated by pH/ionic strength - Steric hindrance from adsorbed macromolecules Destabilization mechanisms: - Coalescence due to film rupture and drainage of aqueous films - Flocculation (droplets aggregate without merging) which can precede coalescence - Ostwald ripening (less relevant for fats with low solubility in water) - Creaming (density differences) which can concentrate droplets at top, promoting coalescence - Protein aggregation/phase separation from overheating Process path dependence: - Droplet size distribution depends on shear history and viscosity evolution. - Stability depends on how the interfacial layer was formed (adsorption kinetics) and on the final composition.

That’s the kind of answer I was hoping for. But now I’m thinking: if hollandaise is oil-in-water, why does it sometimes look like the water is leaking out, like a watery puddle with greasy stuff floating? Shouldn’t oil leak out instead?

In a broken hollandaise you can see both because separation isn’t always clean. What you’re seeing is: - The emulsion collapses, releasing free oil (which floats). - The remaining phase may be a thin aqueous/protein mixture that can look like “water leaking out.” If proteins have aggregated or if the emulsion structure collapses, water can drain out of the droplet network (syneresis-like behavior). So it’s not that water is the dispersed phase; it’s that the emulsion’s structure was holding water in a thick matrix, and once the structure fails, water drains and pools. Meanwhile oil also separates. The visual can be “watery puddle + oil slick” with some clumps in between.
Is there any role of shear-thinning or non-Newtonian behavior here? Hollandaise feels like it changes thickness as you whisk.

Yes. Concentrated emulsions are often non-Newtonian: - Shear-thinning: under whisking or stirring, the apparent viscosity decreases because droplet structures align or break apart temporarily, allowing flow. When shear stops, the structure rebuilds and it thickens again. - Thixotropy: time-dependent shear-thinning; viscosity decreases under sustained shear and recovers slowly when shear stops. Hollandaise can exhibit both because: - Droplet networks jam at rest - Protein-thickened continuous phase contributes to structure - Temperature changes during whisking also affect viscosity This matters for stability because a sauce that seems “too thick” at rest may become workable under shear; conversely, once you stop whisking, localized separation can begin if it’s borderline.
That also explains why it breaks when I stop whisking for like 10 seconds to answer someone. Annoying. Is that because droplets cream and collide at the surface?

Exactly. When you stop: - Heat continues at the bowl boundary → local overheating risk - Droplets can cream upward (oil droplets are less dense than water) - The packed layer at the top increases droplet contact → coalescence risk - If the continuous phase is thin, aqueous films drain faster between droplets So a brief pause can matter if you’re near the stability edge.
Last question for now: if you had to summarize the “science of why hollandaise fails” in a tight paragraph, what would it be?

Hollandaise fails when the balance between droplet creation/stabilization and droplet collision/coalescence is lost. It’s a warm, concentrated oil-in-water emulsion where egg yolk lecithin and proteins must rapidly coat newly formed butterfat droplets and where the aqueous phase must remain sufficiently abundant and viscous to keep droplets separated. Adding fat too fast, running too hot, or running too dry increases collision frequency and weakens the interfacial film (or triggers protein aggregation), so droplets merge, the fine droplet network collapses, viscosity drops, and free oil separates—often abruptly because coalescence accelerates once it starts.
Good. I’m still frustrated, but at least now it’s rational frustration instead of mystical sauce failure. I’ll run those mini-batch tests and see which variable is killing me most often.
My sourdough starter has been going for a week but it’s barely bubbling. What’s happening biologically?

A sourdough starter is basically an ecosystem you’re trying to coax into a stable, self-renewing community dominated by (1) wild yeasts and (2) lactic acid bacteria (LAB). When it’s barely bubbling after a week, it usually means one or more of these are true biologically: 1) You’re still in early succession In the first several days, microbes that tolerate oxygen and a wide pH range often bloom first—lots of them aren’t great gas producers. As acidity rises, the community shifts toward acid-tolerant LAB (like Lactobacillus-related genera, though taxonomy is changing) and sourdough-adapted yeasts. If that shift is slow, you can see “quiet” starter. 2) The yeast population is lagging behind LAB LAB can acidify and produce some CO₂, but the dramatic rise is mostly yeast CO₂ trapped in a gluten network (or at least trapped in the slurry). If LAB outpace yeast early, you’ll smell sour but not see much lift. 3) Temperature and feeding regime are selecting the wrong growth rates Yeasts and LAB have different optimal temperatures and respond differently to refreshment ratios. Too cold slows everything. Too warm can favor certain LAB and suppress yeast or cause rapid acid accumulation that stresses yeast. 4) Flour and water choices influence nutrient availability Flour provides microbes and minerals; whole grain tends to carry more microbes and nutrients (ash/minerals) than refined flour. Water chemistry (chlorine/chloramine, hardness) can inhibit or alter growth. 5) You might be mistaking “activity” for “rise” Bubbles can happen without much rise if the mixture is too liquid, if there’s no structure to trap gas, or if it’s collapsing between checks. To troubleshoot precisely, I need your exact process. Can you tell me: - Flour type(s) you’re using (white AP/bread, whole wheat, rye, etc.) - Feeding schedule and ratio (e.g., 1:1:1 by weight? how much retained/discarded?) - Hydration (is it a thick paste or pourable batter?) - Temperature where it sits (approx °C/°F) - Water source (tap filtered? any chlorine smell?) - What it smells like over time (fruity, sour, cheesy, acetone, etc.) - Any rise at all (even 10–20%) and how long after feeding you check?

I’m using mostly all-purpose flour, unbleached. I started with 50g flour and 50g water. Every day I’ve been discarding half and adding 50g flour and 50g water. It sits on my counter around 68°F. Water is tap, no filter. Smell went from kind of stinky/cheesy around day 3 to more sour and a little fruity. It bubbles a bit on top but doesn’t double. Maybe it rises 10% sometimes then falls.

That progression (stinky/cheesy → sour/fruity) is actually a classic sign you’re moving through microbial succession normally. Day 2–4 often features bacteria that produce unpleasant aromas (some are enteric/Leuconostoc-type early colonizers). As acidity increases, those drop out and LAB take over, and then yeast catches up. What’s likely happening: at 68°F with a 1:1:1-ish refreshment (though your method is a bit different), yeast growth is slow, and the starter is relatively liquid (100% hydration) so it can bubble without holding a strong dome. Also, discarding “half” by volume/eyeballing can drift your inoculation ratio. Let’s quantify your feeding: If you start with 100g starter (50 flour + 50 water), discard half → 50g starter remains. Add 50g water + 50g flour → total 150g. That’s effectively a feed ratio of about 1:1:1 (starter:water:flour by weight) only if your remaining starter is exactly 50g and at 100% hydration. That’s fine in principle, but at 68°F it may take 12–24 hours to peak, and if you feed again before it peaks, you keep diluting the yeast before it builds. Two big levers to get yeast to “show”: 1) Feed based on peak, not the clock (at least temporarily) If it’s rising only 10% and falling, it may be peaking and collapsing when you’re not watching, or it may be underpowered. Track it in a narrow jar with a rubber band at the level right after feeding. Check every few hours for a day. If it peaks at, say, 18 hours, then daily feeding is okay; if it peaks at 30 hours, daily feeding is too frequent and will keep it weak. 2) Increase temperature modestly 68°F is workable but slow. Many sourdough microbes are much more active around 75–80°F. Even moving it to a warmer spot (top of fridge, near but not on a warm appliance, turned-off oven with the light on if it stays ~78°F) can cut the time to peak dramatically. Also, tap water: chlorine can inhibit microbes. Many municipal supplies use chloramine, which doesn’t evaporate quickly. This can slow establishment. Easiest test: let a jar of water sit uncovered overnight (helps chlorine, not chloramine) or use filtered/bottled water for a week and see if activity improves. A structured plan for the next 3–5 days: - Switch to feeding by weight precisely. - Use 1:2:2 (starter:water:flour) once it’s reliably rising; but right now, to build population, you can do 1:1:1 but only when it has peaked or at least shows consistent rise. - Consider adding 10–20% whole rye or whole wheat flour for a few feeds (e.g., 40g AP + 10g rye). Rye is rich in minerals and enzymes and often accelerates fermentation. Before I prescribe a specific schedule, can you answer: - Does the starter ever get noticeably foamy throughout, or just a few surface bubbles? - What’s the consistency after mixing (pancake batter, thick paste, etc.)? - Do you keep a lid tight or loosely covered?

It’s like thick pancake batter. I stir it smooth. I keep the lid screwed on but not super tight. It gets some bubbles throughout but mostly near the top and sides. I haven’t watched it hourly; I just check morning and night.

Thick pancake batter at 100% hydration with AP flour can still be a bit “slack,” so CO₂ escapes easily and you won’t see dramatic volume increase until the microbial activity is stronger. Also, checking only morning/night can miss the peak—especially once it speeds up. Biology-wise, here’s what’s probably happening in your jar each cycle: - Right after feeding, pH is relatively higher, sugars are available (maltose from flour starch breakdown plus small sugars), oxygen is present from stirring. - Early on, LAB start consuming sugars, producing lactic acid (and sometimes acetic acid), lowering pH. Some LAB also produce CO₂ (heterofermentative pathways), but not typically enough to double a slurry. - Yeasts multiply more slowly at your temperature; they begin fermenting sugars into CO₂ + ethanol. As yeast population increases, CO₂ production ramps up and you get the rise. - Once sugars are depleted and acidity rises, yeast activity slows, the structure weakens, and it collapses. To make the rise easier to see and to support yeast: 1) Use a smaller jar and a stiffer mix for a bit Try 1:1:1 but at ~80–90% hydration instead of 100% for a few feeds: e.g., 25g starter + 25g water + 25g flour gives 100% hydration. To stiffen slightly, do 25g starter + 22g water + 25g flour (or even 20g water). A thicker paste traps gas better, so you’ll see the rise more clearly. 2) Warm it up to 75–78°F Even a consistent 74–76°F can be the difference between “barely bubbling” and “doubling.” 3) Consider a short rye “boost” For two feedings: 20g starter + 40g water + 40g flour where the flour is 30g AP + 10g rye. Keep at warm temp. Rye tends to jumpstart because it brings more microbes, minerals (ash), and pentosans that change viscosity. 4) Stop feeding until it shows some rise If it’s not rising much, feeding every 24 hours might still be too frequent at 68°F. You can extend to 36 hours temporarily. The goal is to let populations build rather than repeatedly diluting them. Observation: If you see bubbles mostly at the top/sides, it can mean activity is happening but the mixture is separating or gas is escaping upward. Stirring down once mid-cycle can redistribute microbes and nutrients, but don’t overdo it; you want to see the rise pattern. If you can do one “diagnostic day,” do this: - Feed in the morning: 20g starter + 20g water + 20g flour (or slightly less water). - Mark level. - Keep warm. - Check at 4h, 8h, 12h, 16h, 20h. Note maximum rise and time to peak. Tell me what you see, and we can tailor the refreshment ratio.
I can do that. But can you explain more about which microbes are in there? Like is it the same as commercial yeast? And why did it smell like cheese early on?

It’s not the same as commercial yeast, though there can be overlap at the genus level. Commercial baker’s yeast is typically a selected strain of Saccharomyces cerevisiae optimized for fast CO₂ production in dough. In sourdough, the yeast community is usually more diverse and often dominated by species adapted to acidic, maltose-rich environments and to coexist with LAB. Depending on region and conditions, you might see yeasts like Kazachstania humilis (formerly Candida milleri in older literature), Kazachstania exigua, Saccharomyces cerevisiae sometimes, and others. The exact species vary, but the ecological “job” is similar: convert sugars into CO₂ and ethanol. The LAB side is even more central in sourdough character: genera like Lactobacillus (broadly; now split into multiple genera), Fructilactobacillus, Limosilactobacillus, Companilactobacillus, Leuconostoc early on, Weissella, Pediococcus sometimes—again, it varies. In mature starters, a common theme is a stable LAB population that acidifies reliably and helps keep spoilage organisms out. Why the early “cheesy” smell: Early in a starter, pH is relatively high (less acidic), and oxygen is present. That allows a wider range of bacteria to grow, including ones that produce compounds associated with cheese/feet/putrid notes—things like butyric acid, isovaleric acid, certain sulfur compounds, amines. Some of those are produced by bacteria that don’t tolerate low pH well, so as LAB produce acid and pH drops (often to ~3.5–4.2 in mature starter), those early organisms get suppressed. That’s why many starters go through a “gross phase” around day 2–4 and then clean up. Also, flour itself carries microbes from the grain and milling environment. You’re selecting from what’s already there plus whatever lands from the environment. The feedings and acidity act like a selective pressure. One more nuance: sourdough yeasts often don’t compete well when pH is high and bacteria are chaotic. Once LAB establish and lower pH, yeast species that tolerate acidity and can use available sugars become competitive. If you want a more “microbiology lab” view, the starter is like a batch culture you’re serially transferring. Each feeding is a dilution and nutrient refresh. The organisms that (a) grow fastest in that environment and (b) survive the acidity cycles will dominate.
That makes sense. What about sugar availability? People say add pineapple juice or sugar. Does the starter need extra sugar?

In most cases, you don’t need extra sugar. Flour has plenty of carbohydrate, but it’s mostly starch, not simple sugars. The key is that enzymes (amylases) in flour—and enzymes produced by microbes—break starch into smaller sugars like maltose and glucose that microbes can ferment. Why people add pineapple juice (or other acidic juice): That method is less about “feeding sugar” and more about controlling early pH. Pineapple juice is acidic, so it quickly lowers the pH and suppresses the early, undesirable bacteria that cause off smells and can stall the culture. LAB that like lower pH can establish sooner. It’s a way to skip the “gross phase” and reduce the chance of a false early rise. Adding plain sugar: It can make things bubble because many microbes can ferment sucrose/glucose quickly, but it can also shift the ecology in less predictable ways. You might feed a population that isn’t the one you want long-term (fast sugar-fermenters that don’t thrive on flour-only feedings). Also, it can encourage excessive acid/ethanol production without building the right structure. If your starter is already smelling sour/fruity and has some bubbles, you’re past the stage where pineapple juice helps much. At this point, temperature control, feeding timing, and possibly a bit of whole grain are more effective than adding sugar. Sugar availability in a mature starter: - Amylases in flour release maltose. - Many LAB specialize in maltose metabolism. - Some sourdough yeasts are not strong maltose users, which can reduce direct competition with LAB—this is part of why they coexist well. Yeasts may rely more on glucose/fructose and other sugars released during fermentation. So the system is a bit like a cooperative network: enzymes liberate sugars; LAB acidify and create an environment; yeasts produce gas and aroma compounds; both contribute metabolites that affect the other.
You mentioned “false early rise.” I think on day 2 mine puffed up a lot then went flat and has been sluggish since. What was that?

That’s extremely common. The day-2 “boom” is usually driven by early bacteria that grow very fast when pH is still high. Some of these are gas producers (e.g., certain Leuconostoc/Weissella-type organisms) and can create a dramatic rise, but they’re not stable long-term in the increasingly acidic starter. Once acidity rises, they crash, and you get a lull while acid-tolerant LAB and compatible yeasts build up. People interpret the lull as “my starter died,” but it’s more like ecological turnover. So: day 2 rise = early opportunists; days 3–7 = transition; after that, if conditions are right, yeast and LAB stabilize and you see predictable peaks. Your week-long timeline with some activity but not doubling fits a starter that’s transitioning but slow due to temperature and/or feeding timing.
If I switch to warmer temperature, will that change the flavor? Like more sour or less sour?

Temperature influences both which organisms dominate and which metabolic pathways are emphasized, so yes, it can shift flavor. General tendencies (not absolute): - Warmer (around 78–82°F): faster fermentation, often favors LAB that produce more lactic acid relative to acetic, leading to a milder “yogurt-like” sourness. Yeast also grows faster, so you may get more lift and a sweeter, more aromatic profile if you bake at the right time. - Cooler (around 65–70°F): slower fermentation, can lead to relatively more acetic acid production (sharper, vinegary notes), especially if the starter is stiffer and/or fed less frequently. Some heterofermentative LAB produce acetic acid more under certain conditions (like limited sugars, more oxygen, and certain co-substrates). But the biggest driver of sourness in bread isn’t just starter temperature—it’s the overall dough fermentation parameters (hydration, inoculation %, bulk time, proof time, and temperature). You can keep a starter warm for reliability and still make bread less or more sour by adjusting dough fermentation. For now, warming is mostly a tool to get your culture robust. Once it’s stable, you can experiment with cooler maintenance if you want a different acid profile.
Okay. Another question: what does “peak” actually mean? Is it when it doubles? Or when it starts to fall?

“Peak” is the maximum volume/height the starter reaches after a feeding before it begins to recede. It’s the point where gas production and gas retention have reached their maximum balance, and after that either: - gas production slows (substrates depleted, pH too low), and/or - the structure weakens (proteolysis, acidity), so it can’t hold the gas and collapses. Doubling is a convenient benchmark, but peak isn’t defined by a specific multiple. A very strong starter might triple; a liquid starter might only rise 50% but still be at peak. What matters is the curve: rise → dome → plateau → slight cracking or flattening → fall. A practical way to find peak: - Mark the level right after feeding. - Watch for the highest point it reaches. - Note the time to peak at your chosen temperature and feed ratio. When to use it: - For building strength: feed at or just after peak so you’re transferring an active population. - For baking: many people use starter when it’s near peak (high yeast activity, not too acidic), but preferences vary.
You mentioned proteolysis. Is that happening in the starter? Does it matter?

Yes, proteolysis happens in starters and doughs. It’s the breakdown of proteins (including gluten proteins) into peptides and amino acids. Sources: - Flour enzymes (proteases) - Microbial enzymes from LAB and other microbes - Acid itself can enhance certain enzyme activity and weaken gluten structure Why it matters in starter: - In a very mature/acidic starter left too long without feeding, proteolysis can make it slack, sticky, and unable to trap gas, so it rises poorly even if there are bubbles. - It also produces amino acids that microbes use for growth and that contribute to flavor (through fermentation and later Maillard reactions in baking). In your case, proteolysis probably isn’t the main limitation yet, but if you’re leaving it a long time at warm temps, it could weaken structure. That’s another reason to feed based on peak and not let it sit far past peak repeatedly.
If my tap water has chloramine, how would I deal with that? Letting it sit won’t help, right?

Correct: letting water sit overnight helps with free chlorine (it off-gasses) but usually not with chloramine (more stable). Options: - Use a carbon filter that’s rated for chloramine reduction (not all pitcher filters remove much; some do partially). - Use bottled water for a week as a test (not necessarily forever). - Boiling can reduce some disinfectants but is not always reliable for chloramine and is a hassle. - Some people use a tiny pinch of vitamin C (ascorbic acid) to neutralize chlorine/chloramine chemically, but dosing is finicky and you don’t want to alter acidity unpredictably. If you try this, it’s more a water-treatment approach than a starter method. For troubleshooting, the simplest is: do a side-by-side for 3–4 days—one starter fed with bottled/filtered water, one with tap—same flour, same temp. If the filtered/bottled one takes off and the tap one stays sluggish, you’ve identified a major factor.
How would you set up that side-by-side without using tons of flour?

Keep them small. For each jar: - Day 1 morning: 10g existing starter + 20g water + 20g flour (1:2:2). That yields 50g total per jar. - Mark levels, keep both at same temperature. - Feed again only when each peaks (or at 24h if neither peaks, but note activity). Use identical flour and jars. The only variable is water. Because you’re starting from the same starter, differences will show fairly quickly. If you want even smaller: - 5g starter + 10g water + 10g flour (still 1:2:2). Total 25g per jar. Small quantities behave similarly, just dry out faster if uncovered.
If it turns out the water is fine, would switching flour help? I only have AP right now but I could buy bread flour or rye.

Switching flour can help a lot, especially during establishment. Why: - Whole grains (rye, whole wheat) typically have higher mineral content (higher “ash”), which buffers acidity and provides micronutrients that microbes need. - They often carry more microbial diversity from the grain surface. - Rye has a different carbohydrate profile and lots of pentosans that increase viscosity; starters with rye can look more active because gas gets trapped differently. Bread flour vs AP: - Bread flour has higher protein, which matters more in dough structure than in starter slurry. In a 100% hydration starter, gluten structure is limited, but higher protein can still slightly improve gas retention if it’s thick enough. - The bigger difference is often enzymatic activity and mineral content, which varies by brand more than by “bread vs AP.” If you can buy one thing to troubleshoot, I’d pick whole rye flour. Even using 10–30% rye in feedings can jumpstart activity. Once the starter is strong, you can maintain it on AP if you want.
I’ve heard rye can make it ferment faster and get too acidic. Is that an issue?

Rye can accelerate fermentation, which can lead to faster acid production, but that’s not inherently a problem—it just means you may need to adjust feeding frequency or ratio. Two practical ways to avoid “too acidic”: - Feed at a higher ratio (e.g., 1:3:3 or 1:5:5) once it’s strong, so fresh flour dilutes accumulated acids and provides more substrate. - Keep it at a slightly cooler temperature if it’s racing. Also, “too acidic” is relative. A mature starter is supposed to be acidic; that acidity is part of its stability. Problems arise when it’s left too long past peak repeatedly, leading to excessive proteolysis and a weak, runny culture. If you use rye as a temporary booster (say, 2–4 feeds), you can then transition back to AP maintenance.
What should I be looking for smell-wise to know it’s healthy?

Healthy starter aromas vary, but common “good” profiles include: - Mildly tangy, yogurt-like (lactic acid) - Fruity (esters), sometimes like apple/pear - Bready/yeasty - Slightly vinegary (acetic), especially if cooler/stiffer Less desirable signals (context matters): - Putrid, rotten, fecal: often early-stage or contamination; if persistent after a week with regular feedings, something’s off. - Strong nail-polish remover/acetone: often a sign it’s very hungry (low sugars), producing more solvent-like volatiles; usually fixed by feeding and/or warmer temp. - Strong cheesy/feet: common early, should fade; if it persists, it might be stuck in an early ecology or not acidifying properly. Given your smell shifted toward sour/fruity, that’s encouraging.
If I do the warm spot and rye boost, what timeline should I expect to see doubling?

If conditions are favorable, you can often see a noticeable improvement within 2–3 feedings. Doubling at 75–78°F might happen within 6–12 hours once it’s established. Since you’re at day 7 and already have sour/fruity smell, I’d expect: - After first warm + rye feed: more bubbles, maybe 25–50% rise. - After second or third: clearer rise, possibly doubling. - By day 10–14 total: a stable pattern where it reliably peaks and falls on a predictable schedule. But it’s variable. The key metric is trend: increasing rise and shorter time-to-peak.
While we’re on science, how do LAB and yeast not just outcompete each other? Why is it stable?

Sourdough stability comes from niche partitioning and mutual constraints: 1) Different preferred sugars Many LAB thrive on maltose; some sourdough yeasts preferentially use glucose/fructose and may be weaker maltose competitors. That reduces direct competition. 2) Different tolerances LAB tolerate lower pH well and actively create acidity. Many spoilage bacteria can’t handle it. Yeasts vary in acid tolerance; the ones that persist are those that can handle the environment LAB create. 3) Cross-feeding and shared benefits - LAB can release amino acids and other metabolites that support yeast growth. - Yeast can produce vitamins and growth factors that help LAB (depending on species). - Both benefit from a stable, acidic ecosystem that excludes competitors. 4) Serial transfer selection Your feeding schedule repeatedly selects organisms that can: - grow quickly enough between feedings - survive dilution - tolerate acidity and osmotic shifts That creates a self-reinforcing community adapted to your specific routine. 5) Physical structure In dough (more than in starter slurry), microenvironments form: pockets with different oxygen, sugar availability, and pH gradients. That can allow coexistence even when one organism might dominate in a fully mixed liquid.
Is oxygen important? I keep my lid mostly on. Should I leave it open?

Yeast fermentation (CO₂ production) is primarily anaerobic, but oxygen can matter for yeast growth because yeasts need oxygen to synthesize sterols and unsaturated fatty acids for cell membranes. In a flour-water paste, there’s usually enough oxygen introduced during mixing and enough diffusion at the surface that fully open isn’t necessary. LAB are generally aerotolerant anaerobes; oxygen doesn’t help them much and can shift certain pathways. Practically: - A loosely covered jar is ideal: keeps contaminants/drying out down but lets gas escape. - A tight sealed lid can build pressure (not usually dangerous at these scales, but messy) and could slightly limit oxygen. Not a major factor, but I’d keep it loosely screwed or use a lid that’s not airtight. Leaving it fully open risks drying and contamination from dust/insects. So “loose lid” is the usual sweet spot.
I’ve also seen advice to stir it a lot. Does stirring help microbes?

Stirring has mixed effects: Potential benefits: - Redistributes nutrients and microbes, reducing localized depletion. - Degasses, which can prevent a misleading “floatiness” and lets you see true rise later. - Introduces a bit of oxygen, which can support yeast growth early in the cycle. Potential downsides: - If you stir frequently, you collapse the structure and can’t easily observe rise/peak. - It can increase oxidation slightly, affecting flavor (more relevant in dough than starter). - It can cool the starter if your spoon/jar are cool. A good compromise: mix thoroughly at feeding, then leave it alone until you check for peak. If you want one mid-cycle stir for diagnostic purposes, do it once and note how it changes bubble distribution.
Let’s say I get it doubling reliably. How do I maintain it without wasting a ton of flour?

Maintain a small “mother” starter and build a separate levain when baking. Example maintenance at room temp (if you bake often): - Keep 20g starter. - Feed 20g water + 20g flour (1:1:1) once it peaks daily, or adjust ratio to match your schedule (1:2:2 for once a day at warmer temps). Total is 60g—small. If you bake less often, you can refrigerate: - Feed it (e.g., 1:2:2 or 1:3:3), let it start fermenting for 1–2 hours at room temp, then refrigerate. - Refresh weekly or when it gets sluggish. When you want to bake: - Take a small amount (e.g., 10–20g) and build a levain in a separate jar at whatever size you need. This way, you discard very little.
When it’s in the fridge, what’s happening biologically? Does it go dormant?

Not fully dormant, but greatly slowed. At refrigerator temps, yeast and LAB metabolism decreases substantially.
